did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), specifically a VGG-16 model, for the binary classification of multiple-lesion skin diseases (acne, rosacea, psoriasis, eczema, and cutaneous T-cell lymphoma) using clinical images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'ORIGINAL RESEARCH' and describes a retrospective study where a VGG-16 convolutional neural network was trained and validated on a dataset of 16,543 clinical images to classify multiple-lesion skin diseases. It presents original experimental results, including sensitivity, specificity, and AUC metrics, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning for Diagnostic Binary Classification of Multiple-Lesion Skin Diseases
brief_summary: This study developed a convolutional neural network model to assist in the binary classification of multiple-lesion skin diseases, specifically acne, rosacea, psoriasis, eczema, and cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. The VGG-16 model was trained on 16,543 non-standardized clinical images collected from a Danish population database and evaluated using a test set split. The best-performing model, VGG-16P, achieved sensitivities of 85.42% for acne vs. rosacea, 74.29% for CTCL vs. eczema, and 81.79% for psoriasis vs. eczema. These diagnostic accuracy rates were found to be equal to or superior to those reported for general practitioners with dermatological training. The results suggest that computer-aided diagnostic models based on CNNs may be effectively employed for diagnosing multiple-lesion skin diseases in primary care settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the final dataset with 790 patients; used in binary classification task against eczema.
Included in the final dataset with 870 patients; used as the comparator class in tasks against psoriasis and CTCL.
Included in the final dataset with 157 patients; diagnosis was histologically verified; used in binary classification task against eczema.
Included in the final dataset with 131 patients; used in binary classification task against rosacea.
Included in the final dataset with 394 patients; used in binary classification task against acne.

Mentioned in the introduction as an example of a single-lesion skin disease for which CAD models have been developed, but not included in the study's dataset or classification tasks.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories for multiple-lesion skin diseases (acne vs. rosacea, psoriasis vs. eczema, and CTCL vs. eczema)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is 'binary classification of multiple-lesion skin diseases' using a VGG-16 convolutional neural network. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels (e.g., acne or rosacea) rather than continuous values. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, AUC, and accuracy metrics for these binary classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Aarhus University Hospital (AUH) Clinical Image Database
datasets_used.description: A retrospective collection of non-standardized clinical images from a Danish population attending a single dermatological department (Department of Dermatology, Aarhus University Hospital, Denmark). The images were collected for clinical reference (disease monitoring and plenum discussion) and include photos taken by clinical photographers, healthcare workers, and patients themselves. The dataset primarily comprises patients with Fitzpatrick skin types II and III. It was cleansed of non-skin images (e.g., patient ID slips) using a simple CNN.

use.training: Used to train the VGG-16 convolutional neural network models (VGG-16P, VGG-16N, VGG-16PS, VGG-16NS) with data augmentation applied randomly.
use.validation: Used as a validation set (10% of total data) during model development.
use.testing: Used as a test set (10% of total data) to evaluate final model performance (sensitivity, specificity, AUC, accuracy). Patients were split such that images from the same patient did not appear in both training and testing sets.
use.other: The dataset was also used to cleanse non-skin images using a separate simple CNN trained on 200 skin and 200 non-skin images, tested on 150 images with 99% accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty of sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), negative predictive value (NPV), AUC, and accuracy metrics for the VGG-16P model and other model variants across the three binary classification tasks.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Statistical uncertainty was estimated using 95% confidence intervals for sensitivity, specificity, AUC, and accuracy metrics.

Aarhus University Hospital (AUH) Clinica.split: Test Set (10% of total data, 1,654 images)
Aarhus University Hospital (AUH) Clinica.evaluation_setting: Binary classification tasks using multi-task learning models

comparison_of_models.note: VGG-16P (pre-trained, no STN) was the best performing model overall. Pre-trained models were superior to non-pre-trained models. VGG-16P outperformed VGG-16N. VGG-16PS (pre-trained with STN) was superior to VGG-16NS (non-pre-trained with STN) regarding accuracy on all tasks and overall AUC. VGG-16P was slightly superior to VGG-16PS.

Aarhus University Hospital (AUH) Clinica.notes: All results are based on cropped images of the test set. The best performance was obtained by VGG-16P when distinguishing acne from rosacea. The performance rates were equal or superior to those reported for general practitioners with dermatological training (48-77%).

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained parameters from the ILSVRC (ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge) dataset were used for the VGG-16P and VGG-16PS models. The VGG-16N and VGG-16NS models were trained from scratch with no pre-trained parameters.
architecture_used.input_size: Non-standardized clinical images of varying resolutions; specific input dimensions not reported.

variants.name: VGG-16P
variants.description: VGG-16 with frozen pre-trained convolutional layers and new randomly initialized fully connected layers and sigmoid layers.

variants.name: VGG-16N
variants.description: VGG-16 with no frozen layers and no pre-trained layers (trained from scratch).

variants.name: VGG-16PS
variants.description: VGG-16 with pre-trained parameters and a Spatial Transformer Network (STN). The STN localization network consisted of two convolutional layers.

variants.name: VGG-16NS
variants.description: VGG-16 with no pre-trained parameters and a Spatial Transformer Network (STN). The STN localization network consisted of two convolutional layers.

architecture_details.output_layer: Sigmoid layers (replacing the original softmax layers for binary classification tasks).

training_details.hyperparameters: Referenced in Supplementary Table 2 (not provided in context)
training_details.training_schedule: Multi-task learning for binary classification of five diseases; specific epochs or learning rate schedules not reported in the provided text.
training_details.data_augmentation: Random zooming in or out, flipping vertically or horizontally, rotating, or shifting.

classification_or_output_head.details: Fully connected layers and softmax layers were removed and replaced by new randomly initialized, fully connected layers and sigmoid layers for binary classification.

preprocessing_techniques: Cleansing of non-skin images (e.g., patient ID slips, skin sensitizers) using a simple CNN model trained on 200 skin and 200 non-skin images, achieving 99% accuracy on a test set of 150 images.

Cropping of noise (clothes, background, jewelry) using K-means clustering based on pixel hue values (K=2 clusters) to segment skin from non-skin regions, with an image-dependent ratio lower bound of 0.1 and upper bound of 0.3 to prevent under/over cropping.
Data augmentation applied randomly to the training set, including random zooming in or out, vertical or horizontal flipping, rotation, and shifting.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied randomly to training images to duplicate samples; specific ranges or probabilities not reported.
Applied randomly to training images to duplicate samples; specific probabilities not reported.
Applied randomly to training images to duplicate samples; specific angles or probabilities not reported.
Applied randomly to training images to duplicate samples; specific distances or probabilities not reported.

conclusions: The VGG-16P model achieved sensitivities of 85.42% for acne vs. rosacea, 74.29% for CTCL vs. eczema, and 81.79% for psoriasis vs. eczema, with specificities of 89.53%, 84.09%, and 73.57% respectively.
The diagnostic accuracy rates of the deep learning model were equal to or superior to those reported for general practitioners with dermatological training (48–77%).
Pre-trained models (VGG-16P and VGG-16PS) demonstrated superior performance compared to models trained from scratch (VGG-16N and VGG-16NS).
The VGG-16 architecture without a Spatial Transformer Network (STN) was slightly superior to the version with an STN, with VGG-16P outperforming VGG-16PS.
Computer-aided diagnostic models based on convolutional neural networks may potentially be employed for diagnosing multiple-lesion skin diseases in primary care settings.

The study acknowledges limitations including potential overfitting due to small datasets for acne, rosacea, and CTCL, selection bias inherent in retrospective clinical databases, and racial bias as the data consisted primarily of Fitzpatrick skin types II and III.
The inclusion of healthy skin and non-skin images, which were cleansed with 99% accuracy, may have led to an underestimation of the model's true performance.
important_remarks: The study suffers from racial bias as the dataset consisted primarily of patients with Fitzpatrick skin types II and III, limiting generalizability to other skin tones.
There is a risk of overfitting due to small dataset sizes for specific diseases, particularly acne (131 patients), rosacea (394 patients), and CTCL (157 patients).
The inclusion of healthy skin images (estimated at 3.7% of the material) and non-skin images may have led to an underestimation of the model's true diagnostic performance.
The dataset is retrospective and sourced from a single Danish dermatological department, introducing potential selection bias inherent to clinical databases.
The study lacks external validation; no external dataset was available for the selected disease categories to test for unknown biases.

The data consists of non-standardized clinical images (varying resolutions, backgrounds, and capture devices) rather than standardized dermatoscopic images, which limits direct comparison with CAD models trained on high-quality standardized imagery.
The grouping of multiple ICD-10 codes into major disease categories may result in overfitting, as some subtypes within these categories have less similar morphology than others.
The study did not conduct a quantification of unknown biases, which is a noted limitation.
The dataset is not publicly available due to European General Data Protection Regulation restrictions, preventing independent verification and external testing.
